title: The Role of Education: Sociological Perspectives
examTip: Always connect the perspective to a specific named sociologist with a date — 'Durkheim (1903) argued that education creates social solidarity by…' scores more than 'Functionalists think education is good for society.'
introTips: Know Durkheim's two functions of education: social solidarity AND preparation for the division of labour.
The 'correspondence principle' (Bowles and Gintis, 1976) is central to the Marxist view — learn how schools mirror workplaces.
Be able to contrast Functionalism (education is good for everyone) with Marxism (education serves the ruling class).
intro: Why does society bother with school? It's not just about learning to read and add up. Sociologists have argued for over a century that education is one of the most powerful institutions in society — shaping who we become, what jobs we get, and how we understand our place in the world. But they disagree sharply on whose interests education actually serves: is it a fair system that develops talent and creates a shared society, or a tool that reproduces inequality and keeps the working class in their place?

keyPoints.heading: Functionalist view of education
keyPoints.content: Émile Durkheim (1903) argued education performs two vital functions for society. First, social solidarity: education transmits the shared culture, values, and norms of society — especially through subjects like history and collective rituals such as assemblies, school sports, and national celebrations. This creates social cohesion and a sense of belonging to something larger than the individual. Second, preparation for the division of labour: education provides the diverse, specialised skills that the modern economy requires — not everyone can do every job; education sorts and trains people for different roles. Talcott Parsons (1961) added that school acts as a bridge between family and wider society: in the family, children are treated on particularistic standards (who you are — your parents' status gives you special treatment) and ascribed status. School operates on universalistic standards (the same rules and exams for all) and meritocracy (status achieved through effort and ability). This prepares children for adult life where they are judged on what they achieve, not their background. Davis and Moore (1945) argued meritocracy is functionally necessary — it ensures the most talented people rise to the most important and difficult positions in society. Criticisms: Is meritocracy real? Evidence shows social class background predicts educational outcomes far more than ability alone (Sutton Trust). Education may reproduce, not dissolve, inequality.
writeBullets: Durkheim (1903): social solidarity — transmits shared culture and values; creates social cohesion
Durkheim: prepares for division of labour — provides diverse specialist skills the economy needs
Parsons (1961): school bridges family (particularistic, ascribed) and society (universalistic, achieved)
Meritocracy: everyone judged by same standards — effort and ability determine outcomes, not background
Davis and Moore (1945): meritocracy ensures most able rise to most important positions
Criticism: social class predicts outcomes more than ability — meritocracy is more myth than reality
keyPoints.memTip: Durkheim's two functions: SOLD = SOcial solidarity + Labour (division of labour). Parsons adds: meritocracy = bridge to adult society.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often know social solidarity but forget the division of labour function — both need to be stated clearly.

keyPoints.heading: Marxist view of education
keyPoints.content: Bowles and Gintis (1976) — 'Schooling in Capitalist America' — argued there is a direct correspondence principle between the hidden curriculum of schools and the organisation of capitalist workplaces. In schools: pupils must obey teachers' authority → at work: obey bosses; pupils work for external rewards (grades, praise) → workers work for pay, not intrinsic interest; pupils have no real control over what they learn → workers have no control over how they work; the bell moves pupils between subjects → workers move between shifts and jobs. This correspondence shapes pupils into compliant workers who accept hierarchy and routine without question. Louis Althusser (1971) — education is an Ideological State Apparatus (ISA): it transmits ruling-class ideology — the idea that capitalism is natural, fair, and inevitable; that meritocracy is real; that inequality is deserved. This legitimises inequality by making it appear to be the result of individual failure rather than structural advantage. The myth of meritocracy: the same exam for all appears fair, but middle- and upper-class pupils enter it with enormous advantages (cultural capital, private tutoring, better schools). Criticisms: Paul Willis' study of 'the lads' shows pupils actively resist school authority — not everyone is passively moulded; education does provide some genuine opportunity for advancement; the theory is overly deterministic.
writeBullets: Bowles and Gintis (1976): correspondence principle — schools mirror workplaces
Obey teachers → obey bosses; work for grades → work for pay; no curriculum control → no job control
Hidden curriculum shapes pupils into compliant, passive workers who accept hierarchy
Althusser (1971): education is an Ideological State Apparatus — transmits ruling-class ideology
Myth of meritocracy: same exam appears fair but middle-class pupils have massive hidden advantages
Criticism: Willis' lads resist; education does open some doors; too deterministic
keyPoints.memTip: Bowles and Gintis: SCHOOL = Same as WORK (that's the correspondence principle).
keyPoints.tutor_note: The correspondence principle works well as a structured list — school rule → workplace equivalent. Helps students remember all five parallels.

keyPoints.heading: Feminist view of education
keyPoints.content: Early feminist analysis identified a deeply male-dominated education system: male-dominated curriculum (science, technology, classics), career guidance steering girls toward 'feminine' jobs (nursing, teaching, secretarial), teacher attention disproportionately given to boys, and textbook images reinforcing gender stereotypes. Changes since the 1970s: the Sex Discrimination Act 1975 made gender discrimination in education illegal; curriculum reform introduced more diverse texts; teacher training became more gender-aware. Current reality — girls outperform boys at every level: at GCSE (2023), 72.1% of girls achieved grades 9–4 in English and maths versus 64.8% of boys; women now make up 57% of UK university undergraduates. Sandra Sharpe (1994) compared interviews with girls in the 1970s and 1990s: 1970s girls prioritised 'love and marriage'; by the 1990s their priorities had shifted to 'job, career and money' — reflecting feminist influence on aspirations. BUT subject choices remain strongly gendered ('horizontal segregation'): boys dominate physics, computing and economics; girls dominate languages, psychology, and sociology. The gender pay gap and glass ceiling mean educational success does not fully translate to workplace equality. Boys' underperformance has created concern about a 'feminisation of education' debate (Epstein, 1998).
writeBullets: Pre-1975: male-dominated curriculum; girls steered to 'feminine' jobs; more teacher attention to boys
Sex Discrimination Act 1975: outlawed gender discrimination in education
Girls now outperform boys at GCSE, A-level, and university (57% of undergraduates are female)
Sandra Sharpe (1994): girls' priorities shifted from 'love and marriage' to 'job, career and money'
Horizontal segregation: boys dominate physics/computing; girls dominate languages/psychology/sociology
Glass ceiling and pay gap: educational success doesn't fully remove workplace gender inequality
keyPoints.memTip: Girls are now ahead in grades but behind in pay and board rooms — education removes some but not all gender inequality.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often think the gender gap only runs one way (boys ahead) — make clear it has reversed and girls now outperform boys academically.

keyPoints.heading: New Right view of education
keyPoints.content: The New Right argues that education should serve the needs of the economy and should be run on market principles — competition between schools raises standards, just as competition between businesses does. Key policy preferences: school choice (parents choose which school their child attends, forcing schools to compete for pupils by improving quality); rigorous testing and accountability (national curriculum, league tables, Ofsted); vocational education (apprenticeships, T-levels, technical qualifications — matching education to labour market needs); traditional, structured teaching methods over 'progressive' discovery learning; and schools transmitting core British values and cultural knowledge. New Right influence can be seen in the Education Reform Act 1988 (national curriculum, league tables, parental choice) and ongoing debates about grammar schools. Criticisms: parental choice may increase inequality (middle-class parents are better able to navigate the system and access high-performing schools in different catchment areas); league tables encourage schools to focus on borderline C/D pupils at the expense of others; vocational qualifications may trap working-class pupils in lower-status routes; the testing culture narrows the curriculum and increases pupil stress.
writeBullets: New Right: education should serve the economy and operate on market principles
School choice: competition between schools raises standards (like market competition)
Testing and league tables: accountability and rigour; parents can make informed choices
Vocational education: apprenticeships and T-levels to match labour market needs
British values: schools should transmit core national culture and identity
Criticism: parental choice advantages middle class; league tables distort priorities; testing narrows curriculum
keyPoints.memTip: New Right education = MATCH: Market, Accountability, Testing, Choice, Heritage (values). All serve the economy.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Link New Right education policy to the wider New Right perspective on the family — both prioritise traditional structures and market solutions.

tables.title: Four perspectives on the role of education
rows: Creates social solidarity; prepares for division of labour; meritocracy ensures the right people fill the right roles
Meritocracy is largely a myth — class background predicts outcomes more than ability
Reproduces capitalism: hidden curriculum creates compliant workers; transmits ruling-class ideology
Ignores pupil resistance (Willis); too deterministic; education does provide some genuine opportunity
Historically reproduced gender inequality; now reformed, girls outperform boys; but horizontal segregation and glass ceiling persist
Progress is real but structural gender inequality in the labour market persists
Should serve the economy through market competition, school choice, testing, and vocational training
Choice advantages the middle class; league tables distort teaching; may trap working-class in vocational tracks

0.question: Durkheim (1903) argued education creates 'social solidarity'. What does this mean?
options: Education transmits shared culture, values and norms, creating a sense of belonging and social cohesion
Education teaches all pupils the same subjects so they can all get the same jobs
Education creates solidarity between teachers and pupils through shared experiences
Education prepares pupils for the economic division of labour
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Social solidarity (Durkheim, 1903) is achieved when education transmits shared cultural values and norms — through history, collective rituals, assemblies — creating a sense of belonging to a common society.
0.wrongFeedback: Social solidarity (Durkheim, 1903) means education transmits shared culture, values, and norms — through history, assemblies, sport — creating cohesion and a sense of belonging to a common society. The division of labour is Durkheim's second, separate function.

1.question: What is the 'correspondence principle' identified by Bowles and Gintis (1976)?
options: Schools mirror workplaces — the hidden curriculum creates compliant workers who accept authority and hierarchy
The curriculum should correspond to the national culture and British values
Teacher expectations correspond to pupils' actual ability levels
School grades should correspond directly to future salary levels in a meritocratic society
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The correspondence principle (Bowles and Gintis, 1976) says schools mirror workplaces — obeying teachers prepares pupils to obey bosses; working for grades prepares them to work for pay; lacking control over the curriculum mirrors lacking control over work.
1.wrongFeedback: The correspondence principle (Bowles and Gintis, 1976) is the idea that the hidden curriculum of schools directly mirrors the organisation of capitalist workplaces — schools train pupils to be compliant workers who accept authority and routine.

2.question: What did Sandra Sharpe (1994) find had changed about girls' priorities between the 1970s and 1990s?
options: Girls' priorities had shifted from 'love and marriage' to 'job, career and money'
Girls had become less interested in science subjects than in the 1970s
Girls prioritised academic achievement over social relationships
Girls were more likely to stay in education beyond 16 than in the 1970s
2.correctFeedback: Correct! Sharpe (1994) found that girls' aspirations had transformed — 1970s girls prioritised love and marriage; 1990s girls prioritised job, career and money. This change in attitudes helps explain girls' rising achievement.
2.wrongFeedback: Sharpe (1994) compared interviews from the 1970s and 1990s and found girls' priorities had shifted from 'love and marriage' to 'job, career and money' — reflecting the impact of second-wave feminism on girls' aspirations.

commonMistakes: Confusing Durkheim's two functions — always state BOTH: social solidarity AND preparation for the division of labour, not just one.
Saying education 'teaches pupils to obey' (hidden curriculum) without naming Bowles and Gintis (1976) and the correspondence principle.
Claiming feminism says girls are still failing — they now outperform boys academically; the issue is subject choice and the workplace (glass ceiling).
Treating meritocracy as simply 'working hard = success' — in sociology you need to challenge whether meritocracy is actually real, with evidence (Sutton Trust data).
Forgetting the New Right wants market principles in education — school choice and competition, not just 'traditional values'.
examTips: When writing about the role of education, always specify WHOSE interests the education system serves according to each perspective — this distinguishes strong from weak answers.
The correspondence principle (Bowles and Gintis) works as a list of parallels: set them out clearly (teacher/boss; grades/pay; curriculum control/work control).

For evaluation, use the Functionalist vs Marxist tension: 'While Functionalists see education as creating equal opportunity, Marxists argue it systematically advantages the middle class through cultural capital.'
Always connect the feminist view to evidence: mention Sharpe (1994) for attitude change, and the 57% female undergraduate statistic for achievement.
revisionCardBullets: Durkheim (1903): social solidarity (shared culture) + division of labour (specialist skills)
Parsons (1961): school bridges family (particularistic) and society (universalistic/meritocratic)
Davis and Moore (1945): meritocracy ensures most able fill most important roles
Bowles and Gintis (1976): correspondence principle — schools mirror capitalist workplaces
Hidden curriculum: creates compliant workers who accept authority and hierarchy
Althusser (1971): education as Ideological State Apparatus — transmits ruling-class ideology
Myth of meritocracy: same exam appears fair but class advantage is hidden
Sandra Sharpe (1994): girls' priorities shifted from 'love and marriage' to 'job, career and money'
Girls now 57% of UK undergraduates; outperform boys at every level
Horizontal segregation: boys dominate physics/computing; girls dominate languages/sociology
New Right: marketisation — school choice, league tables, testing, vocational education
Education Reform Act 1988: national curriculum, league tables, parental choice
